channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Says He Will Order T.S.A. Agents Paid as Funding Deal Stalls
item.description: President Trump’s announcement came as talks on Capitol Hill over funding the Department of Homeland Security faltered, and airport lines continued to grow.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold and Megan Mineiro

item.title: D.H.S. Funding Lapse Leads to Longest Partial Shutdown in History
item.description: If the shutdown continues after this weekend, it will be longer than any previous shutdown, partial or full.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Wu

item.title: Trump’s Signature Is Set to Be Added to America’s Currency
item.description: President Trump is poised to be the first sitting president to have his signature appear on the U.S. dollar.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The addition of President Trump’s signature to dollars is the latest example of his emblazoning national institutions with his personal brand.

item.title: Trump Officials Investigate Stanford, Ohio State and U.C. San Diego Medical Schools
item.description: The Justice Department’s demands for admissions-related data from Stanford, Ohio State and the University of California, San Diego, reveal an expansion of its higher-education pressure campaign.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender and Alan Blinder
item.media:description: Enrollment in medical programs is generally a fraction of that of undergraduate programs. At Stanford University, pictured, the incoming class this academic year had 119 students.

item.title: House Ethics Panel Holds Rare Public Hearing on Democrat’s Conduct
item.description: Representative Sheila Cherfilus-McCormick of Florida is charged with stealing $5 million in FEMA money for her campaign.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:description: Representative Sheila Cherfilus-McCormick on Thursday. Her hearing was the first time in 16 years that the typically secretive House Ethics Committee had held a public hearing regarding the actions of a sitting lawmaker.

item.title: What’s on Trump’s Mind During a Cabinet Meeting? Sharpies, Iran and Inflation.
item.description: President Trump waxed on about the virtues of the pen, calling it more economical and a better instrument than the fancier writing tools preferred by his predecessors.
item.dc:creator: Linda Qiu
item.media:description: President Trump spent several minutes during Thursday’s cabinet meeting at the White House talking about the Sharpie pen he uses and comparing it to pens used by previous presidents.

item.title: Standoff With Iran Raises Fresh Doubts About Trump’s Freestyle Diplomacy
item.description: A jumble of emissaries — a friend, a family member, a dove and a hawk — on the Iran crisis reflects President Trump’s improvisational approach.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: As the war stretches on, President Trump appears to be casting about for a diplomatic offramp even as he threatens an escalation.

item.title: In Sleepy Town on Strait of Hormuz, War Rages Just Over Horizon
item.description: For centuries, an Omani exclave has been defined by a peculiar duality: rugged isolation and proximity to one of the world’s most important trade routes.
item.dc:creator: Ismaeel Naar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Stocks Stabilize but Oil Prices Stay High After Trump Delays Bombing Threat
item.description: President Trump said on Thursday he would delay an imminent deadline to begin attacking Iran’s power grid.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Kennedy’s Vaccine Agenda Hits Roadblocks, Diminishing His Clout
item.description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s vaccine skepticism is posing challenges for the Trump administration. Top health jobs are unfilled, and a court has blocked his vaccine schedule changes.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s vaccine agenda — his insistence that vaccines are somehow linked to autism, despite a lack of evidence — has always been a bit of an Achilles’ heel.

item.title: Scientists Filmed a Whale Birth. The Surprise: Mom Had Many Helpers.
item.description: The episode, involving a group of sperm whales, adds to evidence that humans aren’t the only species that gets some form of assistance during and after delivery.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn

item.title: Safety Experts Considered LaGuardia Challenging but Not an Outlier
item.description: Regulators, pilots and others in aviation have worried about the kind of runway accident that happened at LaGuardia Airport on Sunday.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi and Karoun Demirjian
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Federal Aviation Administration has been working for years to reduce accidents like the one involving an Air Canada plane and a fire truck at LaGuardia Airport in New York City on Sunday.

item.title: Air Canada C.E.O. Apologizes for English-Language Condolences After Plane Crash
item.description: Michael Rousseau said he was “deeply saddened” that his ​inability to speak French had diverted attention from the families’ grief.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Nick Lachance/Toronto Star via Getty Images
item.media:description: Air Canada’s chief executive, Michael Rousseau, in 2024. He said he was “still unable” to express himself “adequately in French,” despite his many lessons.

item.title: An Invisible Bottleneck: A Helium Shortage Threatens the Chip Industry
item.description: With a third of the global supply offline because of the war in Iran, gas companies are scrambling to assure critical A.I. chip makers there will be no disruptions.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Xinyun Wu
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: Air Liquide supplies helium and other gases to the semiconductor industry.

item.title: Judge Stays Pentagon’s Labeling of Anthropic as ‘Supply Chain Risk’
item.description: The decision is an early victory for the artificial intelligence company in a rancorous legal battle with the Department of Defense.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Marissa Leshnov for The New York Times
item.media:description: The San Francisco office of Anthropic, which won a temporary reprieve in federal court on Thursday.

item.title: Trump Defends His Use of Mail-In Voting: ‘Because I’m President’
item.description: President Trump wants to restrict voting by mail, which he says amounts to “cheating.” But he defended his own use of the practice in a special election this week.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.media:description: President Trump defended his use of mail-in voting during a cabinet meeting at the White House on Thursday, despite having referred to the practice as “mail-in cheating.”

item.title: Sheriff in California Seizes More Ballots, Ignoring State Attorney General
item.description: Chad Bianco, the Riverside County sheriff and a Republican running for governor, is examining votes from 2025 after unsubstantiated claims of fraud.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Eric Thayer/Los Angeles Times, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Chad Bianco, the sheriff of Riverside County, is locked in a sprawling all-party primary for governor in California, a heavily Democratic state.

item.title: Savannah Guthrie Says 2 Ransom Notes About Her Mother Were Likely Genuine
item.description: Her interview on the “Today” show came more than 50 days after her mother, Nancy Guthrie, was taken from her home near Tucson, Ariz.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:description: Savannah Guthrie, left, talking with Hoda Kotb on the “Today” show. It was Ms. Guthrie’s first interview since her mother, Nancy, was abducted.

item.title: Roman Catholic Churches See a Surge of New Converts
item.description: Bishops are trying to understand what’s behind the wave. People joining the church described their reasons as highly personal.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Dias
item.media:credit: Taylor Glascock for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Times gathered data from two dozen dioceses. Each reported a significant increase in new converts.

item.title: How a Healthy Mind-Set Influences Longevity
item.description: A few qualities, including a sense of purpose, seem to have real benefits — especially as you age.
item.dc:creator: Dana G. Smith
item.media:credit: Tony Luong for The New York Times

item.title: How to Straighten Out the Hormuz Bottleneck
item.description: Nations are looking for alternatives to the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Medlock III
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by Kim Hoeckele for The New York Times; source photograph via Getty Images

item.title: The War in Ukraine Transformed Conflict. Iran Is the Proof.
item.description: Open-ended military conflict in Europe could, at some point, intersect with open-ended military conflict in the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Michael Kimmage
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Nepal’s New Prime Minister, Former Rapper Balendra Shah, Sworn In
item.description: A leaked report on the deadly violence and mass arson that broke out last year has put the new leader under pressure to ensure accountability.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Beech and Binod Ghimire
item.media:credit: Hannah Reyes Morales for The New York Times
item.media:description: Balendra Shah, right, with Rabi Lamichhane, the founder of his party, campaigning in Kathmandu, Nepal, last month.

item.title: California Renames Cesar Chavez Holiday After Sex Abuse Revelations
item.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom signed legislation to rename Cesar Chavez Day as “Farmworkers Day,” moving quickly before the March 31 holiday.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:description: Cesar Chavez in Salinas, Calif., in 1979.

item.title: The Faroe Islands, Wary After Greenland, Vote for Change
item.description: Statehood had been a key issue in this tiny Danish archipelago before President Trump threatened Greenland. Now, Faroese voters are focused more on their own economy than geopolitics.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Regin Winther Poulsen
item.media:credit: Sergey Ponomarev for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Faroe Islands flag being raised in November in front of a governmental building in Torshavn, the capital.

item.title: Salzburg Festival Fires Its Artistic Director
item.description: The board terminated Markus Hinterhäuser’s contract early, leaving the leadership of the world’s largest classical music festival in limbo for now.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone and Alex Marshall
item.media:description: Markus Hinterhäuser, who has been effectively dismissed as artistic director of the Salzburg Festival in Austria, in a 2021 photo.
